speaker: interviewee
text: This is a certificate from the Fuhrer and Reich Chancellor to the parents of people who had been in the war. And it is a, an honor cross for parents. My father has a similar one as a honor cross for having been fighting, for having fought at the front of the First World War, the, the Great War, 1914 to '18.

speaker: interviewee
text: The swastika, there. I think maybe we should put this on something. Let's tape it on to--

speaker: interviewer
text: That's going to be hard on--

speaker: interviewee
text: On to a piece of-- This is a picture of my uncle Curt Gaertner, who was a jurist, had studied to be an attorney, and then volunteered for the war of 1914-18 and was killed in 1917, or maybe 1915, in France. He was a lieutenant in, in the German army. This is a picture of my uncle Fritz Gaertner who also volunteered for the First World War, and was killed in 1917. He's buried in France. Wedding picture of my parents, Hanna and Willi Gaertner, September 23rd, 19-- 1923, taken in the house of my grandparents in Mainz, in [GERMAN].

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the house in Mainz, [GERMAN], where I grew up, essentially, from 1930 to 1937. It was a rather unusual house. We lived on the-- what was called the second floor. But actually, we would call it the third floor, where the, the, the window with the curtains-- you see the window with the curtains-- that was my room. And looking out over the other side to the right was also my room and, and a balcony and the living room. And all of them had windows overlooking the Rhine and looking toward the Taunus, the chain of mountains called the Taunus. That, that was my room, the one with the win-- with the curtains. And then, on the right sticking out, where there was a sort of a little nook was also my room, part of the room. And beyond that, you see a balcony where we lived in the summer. It was a beautiful apartment. Picture of my mother, myself, and my paternal grandmother, taken in Mainz, Germany. And I must have been less than a year old, just about a year old.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's name is Johanna Oppenheimer Gaertner, nee Oppenheimer. And my grandmother's name is Juliane Gaertner, born Blum. And little Elsbeth In between. Picture of myself and my grandmother, taken about 1929 in Wiesbaden, Germany. With in the background, you see the French Occupation Army on the march. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Wiesbaden is a neighboring town to-- to Mainz. People went there for a treat. This is a group picture of my class in the [GERMAN] Mainz, taken in about '36 or '37. I am the third one from the left. And the others, too numerous to mention. Some of them have survived. And when we, we had a reunion two years ago in Mainz, and they invited us. Some of them have not survived.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hm. [INAUDIBLE] on them, except for the new life ahead. A page from the of August 23rd, 1940, when my mother arrived from Japan. And she got into the-- she got interviewed by the reporters, because she knew a little English. And she was very happy to be there. And you can read the caption under my mother's picture that she-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 'Broke but happy. Mrs. Hanna Gaertner, another refuge, demonstrated she can still smile, although she had only $6.18 left when she disembarked for the US.' They interviewed her because she knew a little English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me get some of the other-- OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: [GERMAN] in Mainz, built in 1912, burned down in 1938 and proved so indestructible that they had to try to dynamite it twice, before they got the, the dome down. This was where, on the right was the, the sanctuary. And on the left were the school buildings. It's slightly-- the perspective is slightly off in this picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: Differently in marriages being-- Oh, oh! A-- a-- a-- a responsibility, a commitment and obligation. An obligation of--

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a wedding picture, Frank Lewin and Elsbeth Lewin cutting the cake, September 18, 1949 in Forest Hills, New York.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture of our oldest daughter, Naomi Lewin, born in, in January 24th, 1953. '53? Yeah. She is a singer and works-- and works now as a radio announcer of, of, at Public Radio. She has a show on classical music that she produces and, and announces.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Eva Lewin, our second daughter, born February 27th, 1955, now living in Newton Highlands, Massachusetts. Mother of two daughters and president of Congregation Temple Reyim. Miriam Lewin, our third daughter, born January 6, 1958. She lives in New York and is a filmmaker. These are our granddaughters, Lisa Radding, who is 10 by now, she was almost 10 when this was taken. And Amy Radding, who is almost seven. They live in Newton, Massachusetts and go to school there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Amy's on the right, Lisa is on the left. Lisa was born December 27th, 1985. And Amy was born January 18, 1989.
